title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

This line chart tracks the percentage of Caribbean coral reefs containing the genus Acropora from 1950 to 2007, distinguishing between reefs where the coral was merely present and those where it was dominant. The vertical axis measures the share of reefs from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis spans the years 1950 through 2007. Two distinct trend lines visualize the data: an orange line representing the share of reefs where Acropora was present, and a blue line representing the share where it was dominant. In 1950, the orange line begins near 90%, indicating that Acropora was present on the vast majority of reefs, while the blue line starts just below 80%, showing it was dominant on a similarly high proportion. Both lines follow a downward trajectory over the 57-year period. The orange line drops to approximately 65% by 1965, rises slightly to roughly 75% in 1975, and then falls steadily to just under 20% by 2007. The blue line shows a steeper initial decline to 50% in 1955, recovers to 60% in 1975, and then drops consistently to finish below 10% in 2007. Throughout the timeline, the blue line remains below the orange line, maintaining a gap that narrows as both values approach the bottom of the chart. The data shows a continuous reduction in the geographic range and ecological dominance of these corals across the region. Both metrics end the period at their lowest recorded levels, with the share of reefs where Acropora is dominant falling to single digits.
